data.title: Apple: The Walled Garden Built To Withstand An AI Downturn
data.description:
Since my last Apple (AAPL) analysis, the stock has gained almost 20% in price. This analysis places Apple in perspective with the current hyperscaler landscape and acknowledges that in a worst-case temporary cyclical collapse, Apple could be favored as the more resilient equity of the Mag7. Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Supermicro (SMCI), CoreWeave (CRWV), and Dell (DELL) all have the potential to be hyper-cyclical in their own ways, as they are each tied to hyperscaler procurement cycles. Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Meta (META) are also exposed to massive AI capex burdens and are hence constrained by data center expansion practicalities, most notably related to U.S. energy procurement constraints due to grid build-out lagging cloud services demand. In the third bucket exists Apple, which acts as an ecosystem monetizer with AI optionality; it is quite unique in this respect, benefiting from AI indirectly while avoiding the capital intensity that defines data center developers. In this sense, Apple offers a stalwart alpha-generating hedge in an era that is notably at risk of highly volatile moments in an otherwise strong long-term secular uptrend. In what once was a stock I was cautious of due to its innovation stagnation, I have become increasingly bullish on due to its AI-cyclical-immunized operating structure. That said, I am not fond of the current entry point from a price perspective.

Insulated From A Potential Capex Drawdown

Most readers probably know that Apple's earnings mix is extremely defensive, but it is worth recapping so we understand the durability of the investment case at this time of heightened caution surrounding AI growth investments. In Q4 2025, which was reported on October 30, Apple reported total revenue of $102.5 billion, which was up 8% year-over-year; iPhone was $49 billion, Services $29 billion, Mac $9 billion, iPad $7 billion, and Wearables $9 billion (rounded to the nearest billion). There is little direct earnings dependence on AI infrastructure capex here, so in the case of a macro AI capex stall across Big Tech, Apple should act like a technology staple rather than a high-beta semiconductor asset.

That said, we must acknowledge the reality of herd behavior. What is stopping the AI capex cycles (that may or may not emerge, depending on the West's Big Tech leaders choosing global AI infra scaling to offset U.S. and primary international market grid constraints) from causing Apple stock to collapse with Nvidia in the case of an AI semiconductor glut? The answer is very little, other than the potential for market rationalism, which is rare even if value opportunists will spot the inefficiency and buy Apple quickly on any non-fundamentally-related drawdown. A larger question emerges that considers whether an advanced semiconductor glut could cause a recession due to the stagnation of AI cloud services expansion building competitive ROI, but the reality is more likely to be slower growth or a temporary stall in the bear case. Worst-case negative cyclical feedback loops across global macro are very unlikely purely due to advanced semiconductor value depletion and stalled sales, even if total U.S. GDP consolidates. Given this, the imperative for the U.S. Big Tech leaders to seek broad international scaling advantages is paramount, as stalled U.S. GDP is not pretty at a time of geopolitical competition with China. Apple may be one of the only fundamental survivors in such a temporary AI collapse and macro GDP stagnation, which is why it is a suitable hedge right now for defensive allocators, though I am leaning more bullish on a stable AI growth horizon and committing to that thesis with my "activist analysis."

Eyes On The Relativity Of The Valuation

Apple has long been viewed by parts of the market as stagnating in innovation and achieving average growth comparable to more aggressive capital allocators in the technology industry. However, as far as the investment case goes, I do believe it has been overlooked for its worth in global financial markets as an alpha-generating stalwart that rests on highly socially engrained and still remarkably intuitive technology. We can all acknowledge that buying Apple stock is no longer considered a particularly special decision, but it can remain wise given the durability of the market's sentiment for the stock. This sentiment is supported by the durability of the company's free cash flow, which has settled at around $7 per share in recent years, up dramatically from about $4 just five years ago. This allows the company to maintain around or just under $100 billion in share repurchases per annum with ease, supporting investors who are seeking capital security and a shareholder-friendly culture rather than the aggressive risk-taking that comes with nascent competitors or mid-established powerhouses. To Tim Cook's credit, he did well in preserving Apple's legacy, and the stock price proves resiliency with an operating model to match. Apple may no longer be as exciting as it once was, but the legacy ecosystem is still thriving and growing, and that is perhaps what counts most now.

Technically, the price is not cheap presently from a pure sentiment perspective. Shares are de facto a little bit elevated, with the 14-week RSI near 70 and the price high above the 50-week moving average. This may not be the best entry point, but at some point in the next six months, I think a good one will emerge after the price consolidates for a while or, more likely, drops as temporary risk-off sentiment matures through the remainder of 2026.

One note of caution, however, is that the company's forward PEG non-GAAP ratio is 85% higher than the sector median. This indicates that the stock is heavily valued by the market and that a retracement would be fundamentally warranted even if it does not occur due to abstract sentiment factors. By following the 50-week moving average trendline for 12 months, I arrive at a stock price target of $300 per share. This implies an 11% increase in price from present, and indicates why my current rating is Hold, even though the stock may work as a defensive hedge at a better entry point in 2026 (depending on how AI cyclical dynamics are dealt with by businesses in the macro).

Multipolarity May Be A Harbinger Of Valuation Gravity Amid Apple's Low-Aggressive Innovation

Even though it clearly makes sense to maintain a generally bullish long-term view on Apple stock, there is the reality of what Dalio has termed the "changing world order." Though I have been vocal that I believe the analysis of cross-functional multipolarity is not predetermined (i.e., it can be prevented with the correct global macro attitude from Western-aligned nations), in the bear case, a more dominant China influencing global policy and trade over the long term would almost certainly cause a risk-off or "reversion to the mean" for Apple's currently fundamentally detached equity valuation. Again, the forward PEG non-GAAP ratio shows high sentiment, but not a meritocratically valid valuation based on sector relativity. Geopolitics, in my mind, may be the primary, though clearly not only, catalyst driving a major re-rating of sentiment-oriented tech equities. After all, capital flows would begin to view U.S. tech assets like Apple less like sentiment safe havens and begin directing capital toward growth and logical opportunism in the East. This would not be favorable for the U.S., and certainly not for Apple's shareholders. So the onus is on the West's business community to rally behind unified international allegiance and global support for U.S. technological superiority, in which Apple is a central figure.

Looking beyond market sentiment and capital flows and into operations, the potential for hardware and ecosystem disruption at this time is also high. There is potential for traditional laptops to be replaced in decades to come by hands-free devices or more subtle visual-sensory devices that require less manual touch. Evidently, we have seen Apple explore some of these new modalities, including with the Apple Vision Pro, but at this time the results of its explorations feel more like luxury experiments rather than revolutionary cultural permeations like the iPhone was. In fact, the latest "iPhone moment" culturally has been OpenAI's ChatGPT; there is no doubt in any rational person's mind about that. Apple's position as a stalwart ecosystem preserver rather than an aggressive disruptor puts it doubly at risk in a state of geopolitical competition of fading into irrelevancy, or becoming a stock for "dumb money," rather than maintaining market popularity for decades to come.

Conclusion: Hold

There aren't that many tech stocks immune from an AI drawdown right now (if one does occur in 2026/2027), but nonetheless, Apple may be one such rarity. In the eyes of the market, this is a company with immense resiliency and an ecosystem built on customer loyalty and dependency. It is difficult to imagine any such company of this size losing its value any time soon, but early elements and signals of decades-long decline are certainly emerging without the right internal and macro conduct to offset the risks. I remain long-term bullish on Apple stock, but at the current price point, I believe it wise to wait for a pullback and to add capital when, or if, an AI drawdown looks more "immediate." At this time, the AI drawdown is an if, not a when, and its magnitude may be much smaller than some short sellers currently want to admit.

data.title: GPIQ Is Becoming The King Of The Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
In just over two years, the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) has amassed $2.12 billion in assets under management (AUM) and is becoming the king of covered call ETFs. Their strategy of delivering recurring income while maintaining the ability to generate capital appreciation has led to GPIQ producing 35.41% in capital appreciation while paying out $9.98 (25.82%) in income since inception. No matter how you look at things, GPIQ has maintained an annualized return that exceeds 15% while producing a double-digit yield. I believe this is why GPIQ is gaining a lot of traction in the market, as their strategy is much more refined than the traditional covered-call overwrite strategy, as they utilize a dynamic methodology to maximize the potential to generate additional appreciation while still maintaining an attractive payout. Despite concerns of overvaluations in technology and expectations of a December rate cut being lowered due to incomplete data from the Fed, I believe GPIQ is very interesting for income investors heading into a lower rate environment. The stats speak for themselves, and even during the April sell off due to tariffs, GPIQ was able to rebound while providing recurring income. As the amount of income that is generated from risk-free assets continues to decline, GPIQ should look much more attractive, and I believe that capital will continue to flow into this dual-purpose ETF.

Following up on my previous article about GPIQ

In the middle of August, I had written an article on GPIQ (can be read here) where I had discussed my bullish thesis. Since then, shares of GPIQ have increased by 3.11% while generating a total return of 5.86% as the S&P 500 moved higher by 5.44%. Over this period, its AUM has almost doubled as it has gone from $1.28 billion to $2.12 billion. More people are realizing that GPIQ is becoming the dominant ETF in the covered call option space. I had discussed how its dynamic call option strategy enables strong monthly income and capital appreciation, which sets it apart from similar ETFs. I am following up with a new article, as I am still very bullish on its future prospects and feel it is an interesting ETF for investors looking for a blend of capital appreciation and income in a falling rate environment.

Why I like GPIQ’s strategy and how it operates

GPIQ owns a broad portfolio of assets found within the Nasdaq 100 and then implements a dynamic covered call strategy to generate additional recurring income. In a market where there is no shortage of income focused ETFs, it’s important to understand the product and make sure that it fits your investment needs. GPIQ allocates at least 80% of its assets plus any borrowings into the companies that create the Nasdaq 100 and can purchase common stock, preferred stock, warrants, futures, forwards, ETFs, options, and other instruments. GPIQ has a dual focus of generating recurring monthly income while also producing capital appreciation, which is why I have gravitated toward it. The fund managers at Goldman Sachs (GS) aren’t obligated to maximize income, so they can also focus on scaling the options up or down when opportunities present themselves to create an environment where both objectives can flourish. This has allowed GPIQ to generate a return of 35.41% while producing 25.82% in income since October of 2023.

I happen to like GPIQ’s approach toward generating income because it leaves the upside partially uncapped. GPIQ has a unique approach with an overwrite strategy where GPIQ sells a call option to generate income with the flexibility to implement this on a variable process. GPIQ can only write call options between 25% and 75% of the value of the equity investments, which leaves a portion of the portfolio always uncapped, so no matter what ratio they choose, GPIQ will appreciate in a bullish environment. GPIQ can also utilize FLEX options, which are customized exchange-traded option contracts with the ability to offer additional terms regarding exercise prices and expiration dates. The Flex options also provide GPIQ with the ability to treat some of the income as return of capital. Over the long haul the composition of the option overlay strategy has allowed GPIQ to outpace the average annualized returns of the market while generating a double-digit yield.

From a pure income perspective, GPIQ has produced 24 distributions with an average monthly distribution of $0.42. GPIQ has a tight dispersion range, and at the current average, it would produce $4.99 of forward income, which is a distribution yield of 9.54% based on the current share price. As the Fed continues to cut rates, GPIQ’s distribution isn’t dependent on what the Fed does, so I believe more investors will gravitate toward its strategy. Whether the Fed Funds Rates have been high or low, GPIQ’s distribution has remained relatively flat, which is a testament to their dynamic overwrite strategy. Leaving a portion of the fund uncapped will allow the underlying investment to appreciate in value while the income strategy works its magic and continues to significantly exceed not just the risk-free rate of return but many other investments in the market.

When I compare GPIQ to other call option ETFs there is no doubt it’s becoming the King

I compared GPIQ to six other ETFs, which include the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) as my baselines for the market. The peer group I selected for GPIQ consists of the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD), NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) since the beginning of the year. I created a table below that shows how much appreciation each one has captured throughout 2025 and how much income they have generated. I utilize SPY and QQQ to create my baseline for the market and then see where the covered-call ETFs fall. GPIQ may not have generated the largest yield on a YTD basis, but the appreciation on the other side of its investment strategy has allowed it to have the largest total return from this peer group.

There are very specific reasons I am comparing GPIQ to QYLD, QQQI, and JEPQ. QYLD is the original covered-call ETF that has stayed true to its strategy and generates income by writing calls at or close to the money, which caps most of the upside potential. QQQI is an evolved strategy of QYLD that incorporates call spreads into its investment mix. QQQI will retain a portion of the premium it generates from writing calls to purchase out-of-the-money calls so it can participate in some of the upside. JEPQ utilizes its call option strategy through its equity-linked notes (ELNs) to generate income. These funds all focus on the Nasdaq-100, so it creates a good peer group.

QYLD’s strategy hasn’t held up well in 2025, as its actual ETF has declined by -4.5% in an environment where QQQ and SPY have appreciated by 19.40% and 15.57%. When the distribution is factored in, the total return is 7.13%. JEPQ, which is a top covered call ETF, is also underperforming, as the combination of its appreciation and yield has generated a total return of 12.06%. QQQI, which has implemented call spreads on QYLD’s strategy, has a large yield YTD of 12.6% while producing 3.13% in appreciation, leading to a total return of 15.73%, which is slightly above the total return from SPY. GPIQ has pulled away from the pack with a total return of 16.4%, which is comprised of a 9.63% yield and 6.77% of appreciation. GPIQ has allowed investors to have their cake and eat it too, and it's outpacing SPY while slightly trailing QQQ.

Risks to investing in GPIQ

While I am bullish on GPIQ, investors should do their own due diligence, as there are risk factors to consider. GPIQ currently offers an appealing blend of income and growth, but it is going to move lockstep with QQQ. If we get an environment where there is a rotation out of technology, GPIQ’s strategy won’t provide much cover, and the share price will follow QQQ lower. GPIQ’s dynamic covered call approach has worked out well in 2025, but there is execution risk. If the managers misjudge volatility, it could cap more upside than intended and underperform previous results from an appreciation standpoint. If you’re looking for long-term appreciation, GPIQ is not created to replicate QQQ, so you could leave some upside on the table in a bull market. Please understand the investment and do your own research.

Conclusion

I am very bullish on GPIQ from a long-term perspective, as the managers have proven that they can produce on both sides of the investment case. The composition of GPIQ sets up well for investors who are looking to grow their capital to some degree while generating a monthly check that outpaces the risk-free rate of return. As the Fed continues to lower rates, GPIQ’s monthly distribution is unlikely to be impacted, which makes it a very interesting ETF going forward. I believe that the market is going to move higher over the next year, and if it does, GPIQ’s construction should allow income investors to participate in more upside than other covered-call ETFs while generating similar yields for a larger total return.

data.title: The Dark Side Of REIT Investing
data.description:
I'm a strong proponent of REIT investing (VNQ). I invest about half of my own portfolio in REITs and regularly write bullish articles on them here on Seeking Alpha. Some of you may even call me a REIT cheerleader.

But even then, I'm objective enough to recognize that not everything is sunshine and rainbows in the REIT sector.

In fact, I would go as far as to say that the REIT sector has a dark side, which represents a large fraction of it, and should be avoided at all costs.

This is a vast and versatile sector, and just because two companies share the REIT acronym does not mean that they have anything in common. To give you a good example, consider the case of Wheeler REIT (WHLR) vs. EastGroup Properties (EGP). Both are REITs. Yet, one would have earned you a little fortune, while the other one would have lost you everything.

In today’s article, I'm going to discuss this dark side of the REIT sector and how to avoid losers like Wheeler in the future.

Dividend Traps

The unfortunate reality is that a lot of REITs are paying dividends that are not sustainable.

Just in the past few years, I have correctly predicted nine REIT dividend cuts in my various articles. All of these have cut their dividend by at least 20% since then:

Company Prediction Article
Global Medical REIT (GMRE) GMRE
Arbor Realty Trust (ABR) ABR
Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) BDN
BXP (BXP) BXP
Easterly Government Properties (DEA) DEA
Global Net Lease (GNL) GNL
Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance (ARI) ARI
Service Properties Trust (SVC) SVC

The reason why so many REITs end up overpaying is that their management teams and boards are trying to make their company as compelling as possible to REIT investors, most of whom are income-oriented.

They know that a higher dividend may help them reach a higher valuation, which could then give them access to equity markets to raise more capital and accelerate growth. This can then allow them to further hike the dividend.

But, in doing so, they often end up setting the dividend too high from the start, leaving little room for error in case of future setbacks.

Eventually, setbacks occur, the dividend becomes unsustainable, but management teams and boards will often still resist cutting the dividend for a while, knowing that it would disappoint investors and hurt their market sentiment.

This then forces them to take on more debt to fund their dividend, only putting them in a worse position.

Eventually, after delaying the inevitable for a while, they still have to rip off the Band Aid, which then often leads to sharp sell-offs as frustrated shareholders sell the stock.

Therefore, it's crucial to assess the dividend sustainability of a REIT before investing in it.

And it's not as easy as checking the payout ratio. There are lots of REITs with a relatively low dividend payout ratio that still cannot sustain their dividend.

You also need to look at the leverage, the capex requirements, the cash flow growth prospects, and the track record of the REIT.

Overleverage

Many REITs are also overleveraged, and again, this is often the result of conflicts of interest between the manager and shareholders.

REIT managers are generally incentivized to grow their FFO per share, and the easiest way to achieve that is to take on more leverage and buy additional properties. As long as the cap rate is higher than the interest rate, this should grow the FFO per share, granting REIT managers their performance-based bonuses.

But this only works for so long. Eventually, the REIT runs into some setbacks, and the losses are then amplified by the leverage, often forcing the REIT to sell assets, raise equity, and/or cut the dividend, all of which can then permanently impair equity value.

Take the example of Industrial Logistics Properties Trust (ILPT). The REIT owns great assets, but management took on too much leverage in an attempt to grow faster, and here are the results:

History has shown that the most rewarding REITs are those that are conservative with their leverage, as this leads to more consistent results across the cycle and allows the REITs to play offense during times of crisis, often acquiring properties for pennies on the dollar from distressed sellers.

I try to stick to REITs with LTVs below 50%, but ideally, closer to 30%. Some REITs in my portfolio, such as EastGroup Properties and Big Yellow Group (BYG/OTCPK:BYLOF), have LTVs as low as 10%. Naturally, this results in lower yields and higher valuation multiples, which is why many investors aren’t interested in them, but it also results in faster growth, lower risk, and higher total returns over time. The trade-off is well worth it, in my opinion.

Secular Headwinds

REITs invest in more than 20 different property sectors, and some of them are facing severe headwinds.

The office sector is the most obvious example. Vacancy rates are today at an all-time high of more than 20% as a result of the growing trend of remote and hybrid work. Moreover, I expect the AI revolution to only make things worse as it will lead to significant white collar job disruption, reducing the need for office space, and pushing tenants to require more flexible lease terms. This could then make it harder to finance these assets, pushing cap rates to higher levels, and leading to significant value destruction.

But it's not just the office. Hotels are another sector with uncertain prospects. The post-COVID rise of Zoom (ZM) has permanently reduced business travel. It has also accelerated the growth of Airbnb (ABNB), which is leading to more supply, price competition, and ultimately lower margins. Finally, booking websites are also ever-growing, more powerful, and taking a greater share of the revenue as hotel flags lose value.

Most Class A malls are today doing surprisingly well, but as AI supercharges the growth of e-commerce with more powerful and personalized marketing, cheaper shipping, and an explosion in small e-commerce business formation (as it also reduces barriers to entry), I expect more pain for traditional retailers who fail to adapt, which could then lead to more trouble for malls.

Mortgage REITs also have a notoriously poor track record due to their businesses being too heavily dependent on macro factors that are out of their control. Even small changes in interest rates and spreads can make or break their businesses, which makes them quite unattractive, in my opinion.

And there are many other examples…

This is a sector in which you need to be highly selective because not all property sectors are well positioned for the long run.

Share Dilution

Finally, some REITs, especially those that are externally managed, will often issue new equity in an effort to buy more properties and grow the size of the portfolio, as this may justify higher management fees for themselves.

This is all fine as long as they are raising the equity at a price that’s high enough to earn a positive spread on new investments. The problem is that many of these REITs will not hesitate to issue equity even when they trade at a discount to NAV, resulting in a negative spread and diluting shareholders.

This can then negate all the potential benefits of the REIT, as constant dilution leads to poor performance.

I think that Global Net Lease has been a victim of this in the past. Just look at the clear inverse correlation between its share price and its rising share count:

This is why I have previously argued that investors should always start their REIT analysis by taking a hard look at the management team. Nothing else matters if the management is going to dilute you time and time again.

Bottom Line

If you look at my past articles here on Seeking Alpha, you will note that I regularly cover REITs to avoid and others that are likely to cut their dividend.

I make it a point to cover the good and the bad because no sector is ever perfect, and knowing what not to buy is often just as important, if not more, than identifying good investment opportunities.

We, of course, aren't perfect either. At High Yield Landlord, we have suffered quite a few losses in our portfolio lately. But fortunately, we have had many more winners over the years as well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Western Union: A High-Yield Cash Machine At 5x Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Western Union (WU) delivered a solid Q3’25 beat on both revenue and earnings with strength in its evolving digital and Consumer Services segments despite ongoing macroeconomic challenges. The company's improved margin profile, strong free cash flow generation, and disciplined capital returns through consistent buybacks and an attractive 11% dividend yield make it a compelling buy. With intel from the investor day, my takeaways were that Western Union is successfully transforming from a legacy remittance player into a digital-first payments company with significant growth runway, particularly through initiatives like its branded digital platform, Consumer Services expansion, and the Intermex acquisition. Given its strong balance sheet, conservative leverage, and aggressive capital returns to shareholders, I rate shares a ‘buy’ given that they trade with a 21% shareholder yield for just 5.0x earnings.

Q3’25 Results and Investor Day

For its Q3 results, Western Union delivered a beat on both the top and bottom line. For revenues, Western Union’s revenues clocked in at $1.03 billion, down 1% compared to last year. On a same currency basis, consumer money transfer transactions fell 2% while cross-border principal was up indicating that consumers are still sending money on the Western Union platform despite macro pressures. As well, the branded digital business had transactions up 12% and adjusted revenues up 6%, marking eight straight quarters of growth at this pace. While a smaller portion of revenues, Consumer Services stood out with adjusted revenues rising 49%, roughly half driven by the Eurochange acquisition and half by strong organic performance in travel money and bill payments. In my view, this segment has become the growth engine in the portfolio and now represents about 15% of total company revenues, helping to offset some of the decline in the core business.

When looking at Western Union’s profitability this quarter, operating margins expanded to 20%, a full point higher than last year as the company wrapped up its $150 million cost redeployment program two years ahead of schedule. On EPS, earnings per share was up a penny compare to last year and 4 cents ahead of estimates thanks to fewer shares outstanding as a result of buybacks and steady cost control. Both of these measures more than offset higher interest expense.

In aggregate, when we take the fact that revenues are stable and margins are marginally higher, free cash flow of $236 million during the quarter has helped keep leverage in check and more than covers the dividend and capex and helps to support further buybacks. On leverage, with $1 billion of cash against $2.6 billion of debt, leverage sits at 2.6x on a gross basis and 1.7x on a net basis. With investment grade ratings from both S&P and Moody’s, the company’s debt looks manageable, and the company can access a low cost of capital given the predictability and diversity of business globally. Finally, maturities are also spread out with one bond extending as far out as 2040, leaving it in a good position to refinance the $600 million 2026 bond that comes due in March next year.

In terms of some of the things that I picked up from the quarter, it’s clear to me that Western Union’s business is in decline, but not nearly as much as one might think. There are signs of stabilization in several large Americas corridors after a tough summer, with U.S.-to-Mexico trends improving sequentially from June lows. With the Intermex acquisition, the company has moved passed the waiting period under the HSR Antitrust Improvements Act and integration plans are already well underway with the expected close to be around mid-2026.

At the Investor Day, Western Union outlined new midterm guidance and highlighted the company’s efforts to shed its perception as a retail, C2C remittance company. Unlike in past years, it now sees itself as a digital first company with integrations that are more open-loop with room for incorporating digital assets like stablecoins. In the existing business, management called out their expectation for $500 million in incremental Branded Digital revenues alongside an incremental $500 million in Consumer Services revenue by 2028, while the Intermex acquisition will bring a high-quality agent support model and a focus on transaction-level profitability. In my view, with stablecoin initiatives, the company should be in a better place to support treasury management capabilities alongside its proprietary USDPT stablecoin.

Western Union’s digital transformation targets a $1.5 billion Branded Digital remittance business by 2028. To do this, it’s moving past basic transfers to become a true digital-first payments company. Management is modernizing with cloud infrastructure (using AI for faster onboarding) and building a platform that connects senders and receivers seamlessly. The global brand keeps acquisition costs low and retention high. With omnichannel digital experience, retail modernization that supports rather than fights digital, and a fast-growing Consumer Services segment tied to over 100 million customers. Moreover, a multi-brand approach with Vigo and Intermex should open new corridors.

In my view, Western Union’s digital strategy makes sense. With a fully reserved USD payment token on Solana with Anchorage Digital and Rain, this should improve treasury efficiency across global compliance, cash, and account payout networks. Few traditional players can bridge fiat and digital assets at this scale and so it’ll be interesting to see to what extent this can improve profitability on the bottom line as well as service levels for customers. Despite minimal top line revenue impact, investors today will be left with a steady business that has a rock-solid balance sheet with aggressive capital returns through buybacks and dividends.

Declining business? Think again

Management kept 2025 guidance unchanged on the Q3 call but narrowed revenue to the lower end of $4.035-4.135 billion while signaling EPS at the upper end of $1.65–$1.75. Right now, the U.S.-Mexico retail remains stubborn but bottoming, digital and Consumer Services are outperforming models, and cost savings are flowing straight to the bottom line. Q4 should give a pretty good read on momentum in two years once we lap the election media spike and migration slowdown and I expect adjusted revenue roughly flat year over year with EPS beating the Street on share count and margins.

For 2026 and beyond, Investor Day’s $4.8-5.3 billion 2028 revenue target implies just 4–6% CAGR off the 2025 exit. To me, that’s conservative given it assumes no U.S.-Mexico recovery and only modest share gains in corridors like U.S.-India/Guatemala (digital penetration still low teens). When we look at sellside estimates for 2028 hovering around $1.99 (compared to $2.30 guided by management) with barely any growth despite aggressive buybacks, I think the market is being overly cautious (source: Bloomberg). Consumer Services approaching a $800 million level, another $400 million bump on the top lien for Intermex as well as some 150 million in cost savings should provide upside to margins if the macro cooperates.

Risks to the investment thesis

The biggest near-term risk is the U.S.-Mexico corridor. It still drives around 20% of revenues and has been down six straight quarters from migration crackdowns, deportations, and lower send frequency. Even with stabilization at low levels, tighter policies or prolonged migrant uncertainty could keep heading lower into 2026. Second, execution slippage on the multi-year transformation remains a concern. This is especially true for integrating Intermex while rolling out strategic pricing, new POS systems, and independent-agent expansion. Investors have seen Western Union move slowly on big tech and distribution projects before under prior management. Any delays or cultural friction over the next year could push retail stabilization and digital migration out some years and mute the EPS compounding. Finally, and this is simply a feature of their business, is that regulatory and political headwinds into the digital-asset initiatives could be slow. If we were to see a tougher U.S. stance on stablecoins, broader state-level remittance taxes, or heavier AML/KYC rules on crypto on/off-ramps, that could raise compliance costs, slow the USDPT rollout, or cap the margin upside from opening the network to third-party volume.

Dirt cheap valuation and share cannibal

The sellside has been bearish on Western Union for ages with 1 ‘buy’ rating, 10 ‘hold’ ratings and 7 ‘sell’ ratings. It’s no secret that Western Union is not the cheapest way to send money. That said, people still use it because of the number of countries currencies you can engage with and the reliability, speed, and trustworthiness of the network. Its global presence makes it accessible even in regions where modern digital banking options are limited and allows people to send and receive funds with minimal barriers.

Today, Western Union is paying an 11% dividend yield well covered by FCF and trades for 5x earnings. Compared to payment peers that trade at an average multiple at 12.2x earnings, I think this discount is unwarranted for a business that has maintained the same EPS level for several years. As the company continues to buyback $297 million of shares per year (average over the last five years), that translates to roughly 10% of the market cap each year. With an 11% dividend yield to boot, investors are getting a 21% shareholder return yield (buybacks + dividends). Even if earnings decline, buybacks should steady out EPS on a per share basis so long as the company continues buying back stock.

Bottom line

Western Union is not a melting ice cube. Given the soft decline but aggressive buybacks, it is a stable, cash-rich business actively reshaping itself into a digital-first payments platform with multiple new growth vectors. The company’s branded digital momentum, accelerating Consumer Services segment, disciplined cost structure, and pending Intermex acquisition position it well in my opinion for improved profitability. While U.S.-Mexico corridor weakness and execution complexity are real risks, I think Western Union’s fortress balance sheet, investment-grade credit, and exceptional 21% shareholder yield create a rare combination of downside protection and upside optionality. Trading at just 5x earnings with consistently strong free cash flow, the stock reflects far more pessimism than the fundamentals warrant. I’m a buyer of shares below $9 per share.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - November 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 (SP500) pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

The market sentiment deteriorated the previous week, and we saw a rather orderly decline of roughly 4% before the market bounced back a little this past Friday. Then again, this week, the sentiment has turned positive, mainly on the news that the government shutdown may be ending soon, following substantial progress in the Senate. However, it is yet to be seen if this holds in the coming few days. The government shutdown has actually started hurting the real economy, and the economic impacts could be quite bad if this is prolonged any further. Traditionally, the months of November and December are favorable to stocks, and if Congress is able to open the federal government, we may see fresh new highs and the S&P 500 crossing the all-important psychological SPX7000 level much before the year-end.

As the housing market, as well as the health of the broader economy, will depend greatly on what the Fed will do or not do in the coming months, you can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link is provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield.
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth).
  • Payout ratio (dividend per share divided by EPS).
  • Past three years and five-year dividend growth.
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield.
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth).
  • Debt/capital and Debt/asset ratios.
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings).
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price).
  • Sales or Revenue growth during the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95%, but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 3-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating.
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual).
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.).
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score."

Narrowing Down the List to 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry overconcentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 3-year dividend growth. This is to account for more recent growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (3 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: AEM, APH, AU, AVGO, CCJ, CVE, GE, IBKR, ITUB, SAN (10 duplicates).

Appeared three times: FIX (2 duplicates).

Appeared four times: HWM (3 duplicates).

We are now left with 35 (50 - 15) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is one on the list. We remove GE. There are also three others that don’t have the sales growth figures readily available, but we will give them the benefit of the doubt and keep them for now. Now, we have 34 names left.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes FMX, CARR, CI, and AMH. This leaves us 30 names (34-4).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. There is one such name. We remove DKS. We still have 29 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. There is none at this stage.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major:

Finance-Regional Banks: We keep SAN and BK, but remove ITUB.

Financial Services and Misc.: We keep IBKR, but remove MS.

Financial- Insurance: We keep CINF, but remove AEG.

Computer Software: We will keep MSFT.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH but remove KLAC.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO and LRCX, but remove TSM.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM, but remove HEI.

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep MLI.

Materials-Gold: We keep GFI, but remove AEM.

Energy-Oil & Gas: We keep CVE, but remove CTRA.

Utility: We remove NFG.

In all, we removed four names at this stage, so we are finally left with 20 (24-9) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector/segment):

(HWM), (FIX), (SAN), (BK), (CCJ), (MSFT), (RL), (APH), (IBKR), (AU), (CINF), (LLY), (MCK), (MLI), (GFI), (CVE), (LRCX), (AVGO), (IDCC), and (TS).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at our wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is "not" a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Nov. 2025):

(HWM), (FIX), (SAN), (CCJ), (APH), (IBKR), (AU), (MCK), (AVGO), and (IDCC).

Previous Month Selections:

(FIX), (SAN), (TPR), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (AL), (RGLD), (MLI), and (AVGO).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. We are retaining only five out of 10 stocks from the previous month, and five stocks were replaced with new ones.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

After 8.6%, 8.8%, 12.5%, 5.8%, and 7.1% gains (successively) from April to August (a five-month period), the portfolio growth slowed down a bit in the past two months. But it has still outperformed the S&P 500.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P 500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from January 2011 until October 2025. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 15 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

This series was started in January 2023. Since then, we have provided the progress and performance of the series as it progressed. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of January 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

After five months of very strong showing from April to August this year, the performance had slowed down a bit during the month of September. However, it came back with a strong showing again this month with a 7.60% return. On a cumulative basis (since inception), the portfolio has beaten the S&P 500 by a good margin, 102% versus 69%. The star performers for this past month were Comfort Systems (FIX), Amphenol (APH), and Howmet Aerospace (HWM), with all three returning between 14% and 19% last month. Three other stocks, Banco Santander (SAN), Broadcom (AVGO), and Mueller Industries (MLI), performed very well, returning in excess of 10% last month.

This month, we are retaining only five stocks from last month and replacing the other five stocks. The new stocks this month are Cameco (CCJ), Interactive Brokers (IBKR), AngloGold (AU), McKesson (MCK), and InterDigital (IDCC). As can be seen in Table 3, the final portfolio of 10 stocks is highly diversified into many different sectors and industry segments.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not “buy” recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially when the market appears to be overvalued. We recommend due diligence.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

It was a mixed five days for Wall Street, though the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) managed to rebound from last week's sharp selloff by ekeing out marginal gains. A boost to sentiment from the end of the longest U.S. government shutdown and positive trade developments was offset by continued weakness in the artificial intelligence (AI) trade and hawkish comments from Federal Reserve speakers.

The AI narrative remains under pressure, and concerns over stretched valuations and unsustainable capital spending again hit tech stocks this week. The Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ) has now slid more than 5% in two weeks.

Meanwhile, policymakers such as Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack and Boston Fed President Susan Collins cautioned against further interest rate cuts amid the lack of economic data. On that front, however, traders received a reprieve after the U.S. government shutdown ended on Wednesday with the passing of a spending bill, lasting nearly 43 days and becoming the longest in American history. This means that economic data will return, with the Bureau of Labor Statistics confirming a next Thursday release for the much-anticipated September nonfarm payrolls report.

On the trade front, President Donald Trump on Friday signed an order exempting certain food products from his reciprocal tariffs - such as coffee, cocoa, and beef - amid growing consumer angst over high grocery prices.

Also grabbing eyes this week were 13F filings, or regulatory disclosures by major funds of their quarterly equity ownership changes. Highlights included Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) revealing a new stake in Google parent Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), and activist investor Third Point building new positions in Norfolk Southern (NSC) and Union Pacific (UNP) amid the two railroad companies' mega $85B merger attempt.

data.title: The Hidden Engine Behind Zeta's Surge
data.description:
Zeta Global Holdings (ZETA) is quickly finding itself to be one of the most differentiated companies in the enterprise marketing technology stack. Zeta’s latest offering, Athena, marks a pivotal point in Zeta’s trajectory as a company, but also in relation to how AI is going to shape enterprise operations in general.

In my earlier analysis, the focus was on Zeta Global’s evolving ability to drive deeper, more efficient monetization from its existing customer base, powered by multi-use adoption, expanding ARPU, and AI-driven workflow automation, which positions the company for a substantial valuation re-rating through 2028.

The focus now is how, following the latest earnings, Athena is driving a powerful wave of operational leverage and margin expansion that positions Zeta for a meaningful valuation re-rating as it increasingly behaves like an AI-native infrastructure company rather than a traditional martech platform.

The AI as the New UI Thesis

There is an emerging debate in Zeta’s contention that AI as the new UI expresses an even more fundamental philosophy of organizations, which is that the future of differentiation is in intelligent interfaces, not merely intelligent algorithms. Unlike most of its competitors, Zeta’s AI features have, from day one in 2017, been designed into the platform, which provides Athena an immense structural advantage, having an intelligence that rests upon a deterministic data engine as opposed to approximations of probability.

Athena is Zeta’s AI engine that automates workflows, analyzes data in real time, and delivers personalized recommendations to improve marketing performance. In practice, it acts like an intelligent assistant built directly into the platform, helping customers do more while reducing manual effort and cost.

Zeta’s Data Cloud, which is behind Athena, covers more than 242 million deterministic US individuals, in addition to over 550 million international profiles, updated in real time for billions of signals. This results in accurate personalization, accurate attribution, and compliant activation, which current general AI models find difficult to do.

The outcome is an AI solution that is able to root all recommendations in trustworthy, first-party data. For companies operating in a scenario of increasing constraints in privacy regulation, as well as the impending end of third-party cookies, this is a sustainable competitive barrier.

Athena’s interface is very effective at democratizing access to such intelligence. Zeta’s solution makes complex orchestration accessible to people who aren’t tech-savvy, which enables organizations to add more without having to add complexity at scale.

Operational Leverage and Margin Expansion

The playing field for operating leverage could be greatly impacted by Athena’s addition to this sector. Zeta’s adjusted EBITDA margin grew by 320 basis points from the previous year in Q3 of 2025 to 23.2%, which is also the company’s 19th consecutive period of improving margins, thanks to integration benefits from AI-powered automation, which cuts down human labor in campaign management, thus allowing Zeta to grow revenue without correspondingly increasing costs.

As Athena stimulates more Feature usage, the unit cost of revenue growth simultaneously decreases, fostering a compounding profitability trend. The Company’s free cash flow margin is already at 14%, an 83% year-over-year improvement, although a target of 70% of adjusted EBITDA to Free Cash Flow is set for 2030.

Longer-term, Athena is also a productivity amplifier for the organization as a whole. Through the automation of sales enablement, data modeling, and customer support, it has the potential to decrease selling and administrative costs expressed as a percentage of revenue. The compounding of this benefit could drive margins into the mid to high 20% range by 2027, which would be competitive with peer SaaS companies.

Competitive Landscape Outpacing Legacy AI Implementations

The race between AI in marketing technology is gaining pace, but this is because not all contenders have an equal level of ground to run from. The two current industry leaders in enterprise marketing, namely Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE), are essentially building upon an older infrastructure as they implement an additional layer of Generative AI Assistants in front of them.

The capabilities of Einstein Copilot by Salesforce, as well as Sensei GenAI from Adobe, in terms of content analysis as well as analytics, leave much to be desired, but they’re hobbled by infrastructure debt that’s several decades old, which impacts real-time capabilities.

Zeta, by contrast, is designed from scratch to incorporate AI integration, which makes it possible to natively integrate conversational intelligence capabilities during campaign execution. As for Zeta’s infrastructure, it supports native integration of conversational intelligence capabilities during campaign execution, which results in low latency, increased reliability, and closed-loop learning capabilities.

Furthermore, Zeta’s competitive advantage derives from focusing primarily on the mid-market and enterprise segment, without having to deal with the burden of implementing large projects in terms of costs. While an enterprise project in Salesforce could mean deploying in months or even years, costing millions of dollars, Zeta achieves an average deploy time of 50% sooner, along with an average ROI of 6:1, as shared during Zeta Live.

Valuation: A Re-Rating Story in Progress

Zeta’s valuation is becoming more dependent than ever upon the quality of its revenue runway, which provides a clear trajectory to significant scale. Analysts project revenue of $1.54 billion by 2026, and the guidance from management for 2028 of over $2.1 billion indicates strong revenue CAGR of an impressive 20%. At today’s levels, the stock trades at a forward P/S of roughly 3.2x for 2026 and just about 2x on 2028 estimates, multiples that appear unusually low for a company expanding this quickly while lifting margins.

The degree of visibility into Zeta’s future, combined with increasing margins and cash flow, makes current multiples appear increasingly disconnected from where the business is headed. As Zeta matures into a bigger, more optimized platform, it appears likely that so, too, will markets, creating significant upside as revenue compounds in the coming years.

Risks I Am Watching For

Even as Athena strengthens Zeta’s competitive advantages, certain risks to execution remain. The forthcoming combination of its enterprise software business, Marathon, into Marigold raises operational complexity inasmuch as product infrastructure and staff combine.

A slowdown in unlocking cross-sell benefits might slow growth’s trajectory for a period. There is also an adoptance risk because chat interfaces using AI remain in an early phase of an enterprise’s S-Curve, as well as Zeta’s potential exposure to payment cycles of an agency, which may lead to quarterly volatility in free cash flow even in strong fundamentals.

However, these risks appear to be more related to execution than structural problems, and having a strong balance sheet, stable revenue, as well as established margins, provides distinct protection against downside risk.

Final Take

Athena shows how Zeta Global is positioned as a rare AI-native platform that has real potential to reshape and redefine the infrastructure of enterprise marketing. As it refines the usage experience from code to conversation, it does more than merely drive customer engagement but also forges ways to tap into revenue streams.

As adoption continues to expand, and the market begins to understand compounding economics, Zeta’s valuation discount appears increasingly unsustainable. For those investors looking for differentiated exposure to the nexus of AI, Data, and Enterprise SaaS, Athena may prove to be what catalyzes Zeta’s potential into actualized market dominance.
